item.title: Sample Page
item.dc:creator: Minimalio
item.content:encoded:
This is an example page. It's different from a blog post because it will stay in one place and will show up in your site navigation (in most themes). Most people start with an About page that introduces them to potential site visitors. It might say something like this:

Hi there! I'm a bike messenger by day, aspiring actor by night, and this is my website. I live in Los Angeles, have a great dog named Jack, and I like piña coladas. (And gettin' caught in the rain.)

...or something like this:

The XYZ Doohickey Company was founded in 1971, and has been providing quality doohickeys to the public ever since. Located in Gotham City, XYZ employs over 2,000 people and does all kinds of awesome things for the Gotham community.

As a new WordPress user, you should go to your dashboard to delete this page and create new pages for your content. Have fun!

item.title: Privacy Policy
item.dc:creator: Minimalio
item.content:encoded:
Who we are

Suggested text: Our website address is: http://localhost:8888/childs/child5.

Comments

Suggested text: When visitors leave comments on the site we collect the data shown in the comments form, and also the visitor’s IP address and browser user agent string to help spam detection.

An anonymized string created from your email address (also called a hash) may be provided to the Gravatar service to see if you are using it. The Gravatar service privacy policy is available here: https://automattic.com/privacy/. After approval of your comment, your profile picture is visible to the public in the context of your comment.

Media

Suggested text: If you upload images to the website, you should avoid uploading images with embedded location data (EXIF GPS) included. Visitors to the website can download and extract any location data from images on the website.

Cookies

Suggested text: If you leave a comment on our site you may opt-in to saving your name, email address and website in cookies. These are for your convenience so that you do not have to fill in your details again when you leave another comment. These cookies will last for one year.

If you visit our login page, we will set a temporary cookie to determine if your browser accepts cookies. This cookie contains no personal data and is discarded when you close your browser.

When you log in, we will also set up several cookies to save your login information and your screen display choices. Login cookies last for two days, and screen options cookies last for a year. If you select "Remember Me", your login will persist for two weeks. If you log out of your account, the login cookies will be removed.

If you edit or publish an article, an additional cookie will be saved in your browser. This cookie includes no personal data and simply indicates the post ID of the article you just edited. It expires after 1 day.

Embedded content from other websites

Suggested text: Articles on this site may include embedded content (e.g. videos, images, articles, etc.). Embedded content from other websites behaves in the exact same way as if the visitor has visited the other website.

These websites may collect data about you, use cookies, embed additional third-party tracking, and monitor your interaction with that embedded content, including tracking your interaction with the embedded content if you have an account and are logged in to that website.

Who we share your data with

Suggested text: If you request a password reset, your IP address will be included in the reset email.

How long we retain your data

Suggested text: If you leave a comment, the comment and its metadata are retained indefinitely. This is so we can recognize and approve any follow-up comments automatically instead of holding them in a moderation queue.

For users that register on our website (if any), we also store the personal information they provide in their user profile. All users can see, edit, or delete their personal information at any time (except they cannot change their username). Website administrators can also see and edit that information.

What rights you have over your data

Suggested text: If you have an account on this site, or have left comments, you can request to receive an exported file of the personal data we hold about you, including any data you have provided to us. You can also request that we erase any personal data we hold about you. This does not include any data we are obliged to keep for administrative, legal, or security purposes.

Where your data is sent

Suggested text: Visitor comments may be checked through an automated spam detection service.

item.title: Lisbon Concert Hall
item.dc:creator: Minimalio
item.content:encoded:
Lisbon Concert Hall

Location: Belém, Lisbon, Portugal
Year Completed: 2026
Area: 18,400 m²
Client: Lisbon Municipality
Status: Built
Category: Cultural / Public

Situated along the banks of the Tagus river, the Lisbon Concert Hall emerges from its surroundings as a folded limestone volume that echoes the geometry of the city's historic fortifications. The main auditorium seats 1,800 and is wrapped in a double-layered acoustic shell of perforated cork panels, referencing Portugal's most emblematic natural material while achieving a reverberation time precisely tuned for symphonic performance.

The project negotiated a delicate balance between civic ambition and environmental sensitivity, with the entire roof surface planted as an extension of the adjacent botanical garden. Since opening, the hall has become a landmark of Lisbon's western waterfront and has received the European Union Prize for Contemporary Architecture.

item.title: Marseille Family House
item.dc:creator: Minimalio
item.content:encoded:
Marseille Family House

Location: Les Calanques, Marseille, France
Year Completed: 2019
Area: 310 m²
Client: Private
Status: Built
Category: Residential / Family House

Positioned on a rocky promontory above the Mediterranean at the edge of the Calanques National Park, this family house responds to one of the most demanding site constraints imaginable — extreme summer heat, salt-laden winds, protected vegetation and panoramic views that must be earned rather than presumed.

The building is organised as three interlocking limestone volumes that step with the natural terracing of the hillside, their arrangement generating shaded external courtyards that channel the prevailing mistral into natural ventilation paths through the house. All construction materials were sourced within 50 kilometres to minimise the environmental footprint of a remote build.

item.title: Berlin Residence
item.dc:creator: Minimalio
item.content:encoded:
Berlin Residence

Location: Charlottenburg, Berlin, Germany
Year Completed: 2022
Area: 620 m²
Client: Private
Status: Built
Category: Residential / Private House

Located on a narrow infill plot between two Wilhelminian-era apartment blocks, the Berlin Residence navigates the strict height and setback regulations of its conservation area by pushing its programme into a compact five-storey section.

The street facade is composed of hand-pressed dark clinker brick that weathers the same tones as its neighbours, punctuated by deep-set window reveals that frame specific views into the mature linden trees of the adjacent square. Internally, the plan organises living, working and sleeping across split levels connected by a sculptural concrete stair that also serves as the building's thermal mass.

item.title: Prague Swimming Pool
item.dc:creator: Minimalio
item.content:encoded:
Prague Swimming Pool

Location: Holešovice, Prague, Czech Republic
Year Completed: 2020
Area: 6,800 m²
Client: Prague 7 District Authority
Status: Built
Category: Sport / Public Leisure

Set within a former industrial quarter undergoing gradual regeneration, the Holešovice Public Pool reinterprets the tradition of the Central European bathing hall. The building's exterior presents a restrained brick facade consistent with the surrounding warehouse fabric, while the interior opens dramatically into a column-free hall bathed in diffuse northern light from clerestory windows positioned above the waterline.

Three pools of different temperatures cater to competitive swimmers, leisure bathers and young children, their arrangement generating a gentle circulation that encourages lingering rather than throughput. A geothermal heating system buried beneath the outdoor terrace maintains pool temperatures year-round at minimal operating cost, and excess heat recovered from the filtration plant warms the changing rooms and spectator gallery.

item.title: Warsaw Train Station
item.dc:creator: Minimalio
item.content:encoded:
Warsaw Train Station

Location: Wola District, Warsaw, Poland
Year Completed: 2023
Area: 42,000 m²
Client: PKP Polish Railway Lines
Status: Built
Category: Infrastructure / Transport

The new Warsaw West interchange replaces a fragmented mid-century station with a unified transport hub linking intercity rail, metro, tram and bus networks under a single dramatic roof. The structure is defined by a series of laminated timber arches spanning 64 metres across all six platforms, their warmth deliberately chosen to counteract the institutional coldness traditionally associated with transit infrastructure.

Natural light filters through a continuous skylight running the full length of the concourse, eliminating the need for artificial illumination during daylight hours and creating a quality of space closer to a public garden than a waiting hall. Passenger flows were modelled using real-time data from the existing station, resulting in a level-free concourse that routes commuters, intercity travellers and cyclists without conflict.

item.title: About us
item.dc:creator: Minimalio
item.content:encoded:
Architecture Studio

Founded: 2009
Headquarters: Vienna, Austria
Offices: Vienna · Lisbon · Warsaw
Team Size: 34 architects & engineers
Projects Completed: 80+
Countries Active: 12

Studio is an independent architecture practice founded in Vienna in 2009 by Elena Hofer and Marek Zawadzki. Over fifteen years the studio has grown from a two-person office into a thirty-four-strong team of architects, structural engineers and landscape specialists working from three European cities.

Our work spans cultural institutions, civic infrastructure, educational campuses and private residences — united not by typology but by a shared commitment to material rigour, contextual sensitivity and long-term environmental performance.

Team

Elena Hofer
Founding Partner

Studied at TU Wien and ETH Zürich. Led the studio's cultural and public sector work since founding. Visiting professor at the Academy of Fine Arts Vienna. Former associate at Herzog & de Meuron, Basel.

Marek Zawadzki
Founding Partner

Graduated from the Warsaw University of Technology and the Berlage Institute, Rotterdam. Leads infrastructure and residential projects. Serves on the jury of the Mies van der Rohe Award.

Isabelle Renard
Founding Partner

Environmental engineer and architect with a master's from EPFL Lausanne. Oversees energy modelling, material specification and environmental certification across all active projects.

Selected Awards
  • 2024 European Union Prize for Contemporary Architecture — Finalist, Madrid University Faculty of Sciences
  • 2023 Austrian State Prize for Architecture — Winner, Public Buildings Category
  • 2022 RIBA International Prize — Shortlisted, Berlin Residence
  • 2021 Lisbon Architecture Triennial — Golden Lion, Lisbon Concert Hall
  • 2020 Prix Départemental d'Architecture, Marseille — Winner, Marseille Family House
  • 2019 Architizer A+ Award — Public & Cultural category, Prague Swimming Pool
  • 2017 Dezeen Awards — Civic Building of the Year, Vienna Community Library
  • 2014 Emerging Architect Prize, Biennale Architettura — Elena Hofer & Marek Zawadzki

item.title: minimalio
item.dc:creator: Minimalio

item.title: Madrid University
item.dc:creator: Minimalio
item.content:encoded:
Madrid University

Location: Ciudad Universitaria, Madrid, Spain
Year Completed: 2024
Area: 31,500 m²
Client: Universidad Complutense de Madrid
Status: Built
Category: Education / Academic

The new Faculty of Sciences building for the Universidad Complutense completes the historic campus axis laid out by Modesto López Otero in the 1930s while introducing a thoroughly contemporary language of terracotta screens, shaded colonnades and flexible laboratory floors.

The brief called for a building capable of housing wet labs, computational research suites and undergraduate teaching spaces within a single adaptable structure — a challenge resolved through a generous 8.4-metre structural grid that accommodates all three programme types without modification. Shared seminar rooms, informal study terraces and a public café occupy the ground floor, dissolving the boundary between the university and the surrounding parkland.

item.title: Building the Madrid University
item.dc:creator: Minimalio
item.content:encoded:
The new Faculty of Sciences building for the Universidad Complutense completes the historic campus axis laid out by Modesto López Otero in the 1930s while introducing a thoroughly contemporary language of terracotta screens, shaded colonnades and flexible laboratory floors. The brief called for a building capable of housing wet labs, computational research suites and undergraduate teaching spaces within a single adaptable structure — a challenge resolved through a generous 8.4-metre structural grid that accommodates all three programme types without modification. Shared seminar rooms, informal study terraces and a public café occupy the ground floor, dissolving the boundary between the university and the surrounding parkland.

Sustainability targets were embedded into the design from the earliest stage of the competition brief, resulting in a building that generates 40% of its energy needs from photovoltaic panels integrated into the horizontal brise-soleil of the south and west facades. A passive cooling system draws night air through perforated concrete ceiling panels, reducing mechanical cooling demand by a third compared to conventional laboratory buildings of equivalent size. The faculty opened in September 2024 and immediately became the largest certified Nearly Zero Energy Building on any Spanish university campus.

item.title: Family House in Marseille
item.dc:creator: Minimalio
item.content:encoded:
Positioned on a rocky promontory above the Mediterranean at the edge of the Calanques National Park, this family house responds to one of the most demanding site constraints imaginable — extreme summer heat, salt-laden winds, protected vegetation and panoramic views that must be earned rather than presumed. The building is organised as three interlocking limestone volumes that step with the natural terracing of the hillside, their arrangement generating shaded external courtyards that channel the prevailing mistral into natural ventilation paths through the house. All construction materials were sourced within 50 kilometres to minimise the environmental footprint of a remote build.

The main living space occupies the uppermost volume and is oriented precisely to frame a view of the open sea framed by two limestone headlands, visible only when standing at the kitchen counter — a moment of deliberate architectural restraint that makes the panorama feel discovered rather than displayed. Brise-soleil of locally quarried travertine fins control solar gain on the south elevation while remaining visually consistent with the rock formations of the hillside. The house has been awarded the Prix Départemental d'Architecture and featured in numerous European architectural publications.

item.title: What lead to the Berlin Residence
item.dc:creator: Minimalio
item.content:encoded:
Located on a narrow infill plot between two Wilhelminian-era apartment blocks, the Berlin Residence navigates the strict height and setback regulations of its conservation area by pushing its programme into a compact five-storey section. The street facade is composed of hand-pressed dark clinker brick that weathers the same tones as its neighbours, punctuated by deep-set window reveals that frame specific views into the mature linden trees of the adjacent square. Internally, the plan organises living, working and sleeping across split levels connected by a sculptural concrete stair that also serves as the building's thermal mass.

The clients, a writer and a photographer, required spaces that could transition fluidly between domestic intimacy and professional use. A top-floor studio with north-facing glazing opens onto a private roof terrace planted with ornamental grasses and sedum, its floor structure doubling as a rainwater retention layer. The residence achieves Passive House standard through a triple-glazed envelope, mechanical ventilation with heat recovery and a photovoltaic array integrated into the roof parapet — making the house effectively energy self-sufficient across the calendar year.

item.title: Fitting a Swimming Pool to Prague
item.dc:creator: Minimalio
item.content:encoded:
Set within a former industrial quarter undergoing gradual regeneration, the Holešovice Public Pool reinterprets the tradition of the Central European bathing hall. The building's exterior presents a restrained brick facade consistent with the surrounding warehouse fabric, while the interior opens dramatically into a column-free hall bathed in diffuse northern light from clerestory windows positioned above the waterline. Three pools of different temperatures cater to competitive swimmers, leisure bathers and young children, their arrangement generating a gentle circulation that encourages lingering rather than throughput.

A geothermal heating system buried beneath the outdoor terrace maintains pool temperatures year-round at minimal operating cost, and excess heat recovered from the filtration plant warms the changing rooms and spectator gallery. The project was delivered on a constrained municipal budget through a rigorous approach to material economy — exposed fair-faced concrete, uncoated steel and handmade ceramic tile form the complete palette of interior finishes. The pool has since become one of the most frequented public facilities in the district, attracting over 200,000 visits in its first year.

item.title: Little but Big Train Station
item.dc:creator: Minimalio
item.content:encoded:
The new Warsaw West interchange replaces a fragmented mid-century station with a unified transport hub linking intercity rail, metro, tram and bus networks under a single dramatic roof. The structure is defined by a series of laminated timber arches spanning 64 metres across all six platforms, their warmth deliberately chosen to counteract the institutional coldness traditionally associated with transit infrastructure. Natural light filters through a continuous skylight running the full length of the concourse, eliminating the need for artificial illumination during daylight hours and creating a quality of space closer to a public garden than a waiting hall.

Passenger flows were modelled using real-time data from the existing station, resulting in a level-free concourse that routes commuters, intercity travellers and cyclists without conflict. A dedicated cycling hall with 800 secure spaces and a repair workshop signals the city's commitment to multimodal mobility. The station is certified BREEAM Excellent and is projected to serve 85,000 passengers daily by 2030.

item.title: Most Proud of Concert Hall
item.dc:creator: Minimalio
item.content:encoded:
Situated along the banks of the Tagus river, the Lisbon Concert Hall emerges from its surroundings as a folded limestone volume that echoes the geometry of the city's historic fortifications. The main auditorium seats 1,800 and is wrapped in a double-layered acoustic shell of perforated cork panels, referencing Portugal's most emblematic natural material while achieving a reverberation time precisely tuned for symphonic performance. A secondary chamber hall and open-air terrace complete the cultural programme, allowing the building to operate as a civic stage at multiple scales throughout the day.

The project negotiated a delicate balance between civic ambition and environmental sensitivity, with the entire roof surface planted as an extension of the adjacent botanical garden. Rainwater harvesting integrated into the structural slabs reduces the building's potable water consumption by 60%. Since opening, the hall has become a landmark of Lisbon's western waterfront and has received the European Union Prize for Contemporary Architecture.
